skill.name: benchling-integration
skill.description: Benchling integration guidance for registry entities, inventory, ELN entries, workflows, API authentication, SDK usage, and data warehouse workflows.
skill.summary: Build Benchling API and SDK workflows for lab data automation.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but manual review found they are documentation examples for Benchling SDK and REST API usage. Confirmed risks are limited to user-supplied credentials, Benchling network calls, and a local CSV export example; no malicious intent, prompt injection, or hidden execution was found.

low_findings.title: Credential handling guidance uses placeholder secrets
low_findings.description: The skill documents API key, OAuth, environment variable, and dotenv patterns for Benchling authentication. This is expected for a Benchling integration, but users must provide and protect their own credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly accesses environment variables and describes API keys. The values are placeholders and local configuration examples, not embedded real secrets or exfiltration code.

low_findings.title: Network access is limited to Benchling API examples
low_findings.description: The network findings are HTTPS calls to tenant-scoped Benchling API endpoints and JavaScript fetch examples for normal API pagination and error handling. No evidence found of unrelated outbound endpoints or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch and curl calls are direct Benchling API examples using placeholder tenant URLs. Their purpose matches the skill description and no suspicious destination is present.

low_findings.title: CSV file write example may export sensitive lab data
low_findings.description: The skill includes a documentation example that writes sequence export data to a local CSV file. This is not executed automatically, but exported sequence and registry data can be sensitive and should be handled under lab policy.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is a concrete example that could persist sensitive Benchling data if copied by a user. It remains low risk because it is documentation, local, and not hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static command execution findings are Markdown examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The external command findings are caused by Markdown code fences, inline code, package installation examples, and curl examples. I found no executable skill script that runs these commands automatically.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown documentation blocks showing Python, bash, or SDK examples. They are not loaded by code and do not execute as part of the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak cryptography findings are semantic false positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography blocker findings match unrelated text such as HTTP status codes, inventory destination identifiers, or short biological sequence examples. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or custom cryptography usage.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain HTTP status documentation, container transfer placeholder IDs, and retry status codes. They are not cryptographic algorithms or security-sensitive hashing code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reconnaissance findings are API troubleshooting text
dangerous_patterns.description: The reconnaissance findings correspond to HTTP status code explanations, authentication tests, or API error documentation. No evidence found of host, network, or system discovery logic in the skill files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited text documents API errors or normal filtering patterns. There is no code that probes local systems or scans networks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic import finding is a Python import statement
dangerous_patterns.description: The dynamic import finding points to a normal Python import block for Benchling SDK error classes. It is not a JavaScript dynamic import expression and does not load arbitrary modules.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines show a static Python from-import statement. There is no dynamic module path, eval, or runtime-controlled import behavior.

content.value_statement: Benchling integrations require correct API patterns, authentication, and data model choices. This skill provides practical guidance for SDK, REST API, registry, inventory, ELN, workflow, and warehouse tasks.
actual_capabilities: Explains Benchling authentication with API keys, OAuth client credentials, and OIDC patterns.
Shows SDK workflows for DNA, RNA, protein, custom entity, and mixture registry operations.
Documents inventory operations for containers, boxes, locations, transfers, and check-in workflows.
Guides ELN entry creation, entity linking, workflow task updates, and asynchronous task polling.
Provides REST endpoint examples for direct HTTP calls, pagination, filtering, and error handling.
Outlines data export and warehouse analytics patterns for reporting and external analysis.
limitations: It does not include executable scripts or a packaged Benchling client application.
Users must provide their own Benchling tenant URL, credentials, schema IDs, and folder IDs.
It cannot verify permissions, tenant configuration, or live API behavior without user access.
Examples may need updates for tenant-specific schemas, rate limits, and compliance policies.

use_cases.title: Automate Registry Imports
use_cases.description: Create repeatable SDK workflows for registering DNA sequences, proteins, mixtures, and custom entities.

use_cases.title: Manage Lab Inventory Data
use_cases.description: Plan container, box, location, transfer, and audit workflows that match Benchling inventory rules.

use_cases.title: Connect ELN and Analytics
use_cases.description: Design integrations that link notebook entries, workflow tasks, events, and warehouse reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Benchling Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me initialize a Benchling SDK client using environment variables for my tenant URL and API key. Explain each required value.

prompt_templates.title: Create Registry Entities
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Benchling SDK workflow to create DNA sequences with schema fields, folder IDs, and safe registry registration behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Inventory Transfers
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an inventory automation that lists containers in a box, validates destination locations, and transfers selected containers.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Production Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Benchling integration design for OAuth, pagination, retry handling, audit logging, event routing, and sensitive data export controls.

output_examples.input: User asks how to authenticate to Benchling without hardcoding secrets.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains API key and OAuth choices, recommends environment variables or a secret manager, and lists required tenant settings.

output_examples.input: User asks for an inventory transfer workflow.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a step-by-step plan to query containers, validate permissions, choose destination storage, handle rate limits, and record audit details.

output_examples.input: User asks how to export sequence data for reporting.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes filtering registry records, limiting fields, protecting exported files, and aligning the export with lab data policies.

best_practices: Store Benchling credentials in environment variables or a managed secret store, never in source files.
Use server-side filters, pagination, retries, and rate-limit handling for large registry or inventory queries.
Confirm schema IDs, folder IDs, permissions, and export controls in a non-production tenant before automation.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys, OAuth secrets, tenant data, or protected lab records into prompts.
Do not bulk update registry or inventory records without validation, logging, and rollback planning.
Do not export sensitive sequence, sample, or ELN data to unmanaged local files.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to Benchling automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples. Users must configure credentials, tenant URLs, and API access.

faq.question: Which authentication methods are covered?
faq.answer: It covers API keys, OAuth client credentials, OIDC concepts, environment variables, and basic credential rotation guidance.

faq.question: Can it create registry entities?
faq.answer: It explains how to create and update Benchling registry entities through SDK examples and REST endpoint references.

faq.question: Does it support inventory workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents containers, boxes, locations, transfers, check-in, check-out, and related inventory automation patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with regulated lab data?
faq.answer: Use it only with your organization policies. Avoid sharing real secrets or protected records in prompts or unmanaged files.

faq.question: Does it include live Data Warehouse queries?
faq.answer: No. It describes warehouse use cases and access patterns, but users must supply their own SQL clients and credentials.
